note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence pauses (never rewritten, summarized, or reordered; stage directions and pronunciation spellings kept exactly as spoken, e.g. 'four point oh five', 'four point two one'). Block windows are MEASURED onsets, not the nominal formula: Sigma(ffprobe per-file audio + 0.4) puts the s11 end at 214.895s, overshooting the ffprobe-probed master (214.518s) by 0.377s (> 0.3 tolerance) because the concatenated MP3 frames decode shorter than the per-file duration sum, and the accumulated 0.4s gaps drift (s11 nominal onset 195.086s vs measured 194.72s). Onsets were therefore measured by normalized cross-correlation of each WC-PB-10-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM; all 11 blocks matched at score 1.000). The last block end is set to the ffprobe master duration. Master ffprobe duration 214.518s.

phrases.text: Start in Settings, under Customer Portal.
The hub names the portal and, right beside it, one chip does the heavy lifting: Live.
If that chip isn't showing, nobody signs in and the ticket is portal-wide.
It is, so keep going.
And leave Publish Changes alone.
Now the five tiles across the top.
Eighteen hundred forty registered members.
Two hundred forty-seven logins today.
That second number is your triage: members are getting in right now,
so this ticket is probably one account.
Zero logins on a live portal means the opposite.
Next, the address.
Click Copy and compare it to the link the member is using.
A stale URL is the most common can't-log-in there is.
Then open Preview Portal and try to reproduce what they see.
Portal Health backs you up: API connected, certificate valid.
The backend is fine.
Back on the hub, two cards settle most of these tickets.
Access and Security holds the login rules: phone and email, OTP on, thirty-minute sessions.
Features and Modules reads seven of nine enabled.
For a missing feature, that line is usually the whole answer.
Two modules are just off.
Open Access and Security and read the login methods.
Phone, yes. Email, yes. Membership number, no. Guest access, no.
So the member typing their membership number at sign-in isn't broken,
and neither is the portal.
That method is switched off.
Match how they log in against what's actually enabled.
Stay on this tab for the quiet culprit: OTP.
A one-time code is required on every login, delivered by SMS, and it dies after ten minutes.
A wrong or dead mobile number means the code never lands.
The password rules sit just below: eight characters minimum, nothing fancier required.
Back on the hub, read Recent Portal Activity.
One line is worth the whole feed:
three failed login attempts on one account, this morning, from mobile.
That names the member, counts the tries, stamps the time, and tells you which channel to test.
They're reaching the login and getting rejected.
For a mobile-app complaint, switch to Digital and open Mobile App Users.
Forty-eight hundred twenty users, more than half on iOS.
The list below carries the three columns that explain most app tickets:
app version, notifications, and status.
Find the member's row.
Tom Brown is running four point oh five on Android while others are on four point two one.
An out-of-date build explains a login that fails or a feature that never appears.
Notifications off explains I never get alerts.
And status confirms the account itself is active.
Now write it up.
Portal live, on the right address.
Which login method they used against what's enabled.
OTP required, delivered by SMS.
The failed-login line with its account, count, time, and channel.
For mobile, the app version, notifications, and status.
Flipping any of those switches happens in production, never here.
That's the flow.
Read the Live chip and the logins before you blame one account.
Check the login methods and OTP when sign-in fails.
Read the activity feed for proof.
Check the member's app row when it's mobile.
Then route it, with nothing published and nothing saved.
